attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old man presents to his general practitioner with a ‘lump’ on the end of his penis that he first noticed 3 days ago. The patient denies any other symptoms. He is sexually active with both men and women, including oral, vaginal, and anal sex. He often forgets to use a condom, but has no previous history of STIs. He denies illicit drug use. He has no relevant medical history, takes no medications and denies allergies.

His vital signs are normal, heart sounds are dual with no murmurs and lungs are clear. There is no organomegaly or pain on abdominal palpation, however, several non-painful enlarged inguinal lymph nodes can be palpated. On inspection of the genital region there is a single well demarcated, hard and painless ulcer on the glans of the penis. There are no other abnormalities.
attributes.stemQuestion: After treatment with a stat. dose of intramuscular benzathine benzyl penicillin, which one of the following is the next step in management?
options: Contact tracing of sexual contacts from the past 12 months
Advise patient to abstain from sexual intercourse for the next 4 weeks
Report this case to the relevant state health department
attributes.explanation:
This question requires the student to deduct that the most likely cause of this clinical presentation is syphilis. Another hint to the diagnosis is given in the question, with the management being a 'stat. dose of intramuscular benzathine benzypenicillin'.

The correct answer is 'report this case to the relevant state health department'. Syphilis is one of four sexually transmitted infections that is a notifiable disease, and thus, this is the next step in management.

The option to 'advise patient to abstain from sexual intercourse for the next 4 weeks' is incorrect. Abstinence from sexual contact is required for 7 days after the treatment of syphilis, however, it is advised to continue abstaining from sexual intercourse with any sexual contacts from the last 3 month (6 months for secondary syphilis).

'Contact tracing of sexual contacts from the past 12 months' is the incorrect option. The timeframe for contact tracing in cases of syphilis depends on the stage of syphilis at presentation, and the duration of symptoms. This patient has presented with primary syphilis, thus contact tracing is only required for sexual contacts in the last 3 months (plus the duration of the patients' symptoms). These sexual contacts will receive treatment regardless of serological testing results.

The option of 'admission to hospital for monitoring' is incorrect. In the case of primary syphilis, and since this patient is otherwise asymptomatic, hospital admission would not be required. However in other circumstances, and if syphilis has progressed further than the primary phase, this may be necessary.

Therefore, the option of 'no further management is required' is incorrect.
learningPoints: After antimicrobial treatment of syphilis, management includes tracing of sexual contacts from the past 3 months, notifying the relevant state health department of this case, referring to a sexual health specialist and further sexual education.
references: Australian STI Management Guidelines [Internet]. Australia; The Australian Government Department of Health; 2021. Syphilis; 2021 [cited 25 Jan 2022]. Available from: https://sti.guidelines.org.au/sexually-transmissible-infections/syphilis/
